content.text:
Conduct a forensic analysis of the prof's MCQ design patterns and produce a "no-clue playbook" — what to do on an MCQ when you genuinely don't know the answer, by reading the question structure rather than the content.
Inputs:

30-Sources/Computer-Vision/mock-exams/mock-exam.pdf — questions
30-Sources/Computer-Vision/mock-exams/mock-exam-graded.pdf — questions + worked answers + marking scheme (the calibration sample)
40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/exam-blueprint.md — per-question topic/depth classification
40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/exam-style-guide.md — existing distractor-pattern observations (a, b, c — symmetric swap, plausible-but-incomplete, numerical trap). Build on these; don't recapitulate.
40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/exam-hypothesis.md — discrimination MCQ analysis


Step 1 — Tag every MCQ on the mock against three pattern layers
For each of the 25 MCQs on the prof's mock, tag the question against patterns from three layers. Be aggressive — if a pattern appears in 3+ of 25 questions, treat it as signal. Note confidence per pattern.
Layer 1 — Semantic patterns (what the prof is testing)
For each MCQ, identify:

The defining property (the right answer's anchor)
The adjacency family of distractors — which neighbouring concepts did the prof pull wrong options from? (e.g. for "Inception's defining feature," distractors come from VGG, ResNet, ViT — the architecture genealogy)
The conceptual axis the question is testing (formula vs purpose vs failure mode vs when-to-use)

Output: a per-question table — Q# | topic | defining property | distractor-family | conceptual axis.
Look for cross-question patterns: does the prof reliably draw distractors from a specific source (the previous method in the lecture? The next concept introduced? The opposing technique?). Patterns appearing 3+ times get named.
Layer 2 — Logical patterns (test-wiseness rules)
For each MCQ, tag whether the option set has structural tells:

Mutually exclusive opposites in the option set (e.g. "increases" vs "decreases" — when both appear, one is usually right; the other two are filler)
Subset/superset relationships (option A is a strict subset of option C — usually one of them is right)
Odd-one-out (three options share a phrase or category, one differs — the odd one is usually wrong, OR usually right depending on prof's style; analyse which)
All-of-the-above / none-of-the-above frequency, and prof's hit rate on these
Negation traps — "which of the following is NOT" stems
Quantifier traps — "always," "never," "only" usually wrong; "may," "can" usually right (classic Millman rule — verify it holds for this prof)

Count occurrences. Patterns in 3+ questions get a rule.
Layer 3 — Surface patterns (mechanical signals)
For each MCQ, measure:

Option length (character count for each of A/B/C/D); is the correct answer reliably the longest? The most specific?
Grammatical agreement with the stem (singular/plural, verb form, article — does only one option fit grammatically?)
Vocabulary echo — does the correct answer reuse a key noun/phrase from the stem more than the distractors?
Position bias — across all 25 MCQs, is the correct answer over-represented in any position (A/B/C/D)? Pure base-rate calculation.
Keyword tells — words like "specifically," "primarily," "main" in distractors that signal hedging

Surface signals are the most exploit-y. Compute them, flag the strong ones, but warn that they're the most likely to fail if the prof has anti-test-wiseness defences.

Step 2 — Where patterns diverge by MCQ type, call it out
Group the 25 MCQs into the three classes already identified in exam-hypothesis.md:

Recall (~5 questions)
Discrimination (~10 questions)
Computation (~5 questions)
Application (~5 questions)

Where a pattern's behaviour differs by class, note it. Examples to look for:

Discrimination MCQs probably have length-balanced options (the prof has invested in plausibility) — surface tells fail here
Computation MCQs probably have numerically clustered distractors (off-by-one, factor-of-2) — different exploit pattern
Recall MCQs are where length and vocabulary-echo tells are strongest

If a pattern works for one class but not another, the playbook must split.

Step 3 — Build the playbook
Produce three artifacts in 40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/. Pick the format that best matches each piece of content; don't force all three into one shape.
mcq-playbook.md — the main artifact, formatted however you judge most usable under time pressure (decision tree, ranked steps, lookup table, or a hybrid). Must include:

The "I have 30 seconds and no idea" sequence — the ranked moves to try in order, fastest first
Per-pattern rule cards — for each significant pattern detected, what it looks like and how to act on it
Class-specific overrides where patterns differ across recall / discrimination / computation / application
Confidence labels on every rule (HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW based on how often the pattern held in the mock)

mcq-pattern-evidence.md — the audit trail showing the per-question tagging from Step 1 and the frequency counts. So if a rule fails on the real exam, you can trace which questions it was based on.
mcq-playbook-cheatsheet.md — one-page distillation. The 5–7 most reliable moves, ranked. The thing you actually scan during the exam.

Constraints

Be aggressive but not dishonest. A pattern in 3 questions is signal, not certainty. Tag every rule with confidence and the count it's based on.
Distinguish "exploit" from "study replacement." The playbook is for moments of genuine ignorance, not a reason to skip studying. Note this prominently — pattern-spotting buys 1–2 marks of expected value at most; the marks come from knowing the material.
Watch for the Millman caveat. Classical test-wiseness rules (long answer is right, "always/never" is wrong) are well-known to test designers. If the prof has anti-test-wiseness defences (e.g. equal option lengths, balanced absolutism), say so — the surface playbook is weaker.
Cross-reference with exam-style-guide.md. That document already names three distractor families (symmetric swap, plausible-but-incomplete, numerical trap). Extend, don't repeat.
For each pattern, predict where it should hold or fail on the real exam. A pattern in the mock might not generalise; flag the ones most likely to be brittle.


Show me a brief plan first: which patterns you'll specifically check, the order, any clarifying questions. Wait for "go".

I've read the three review docs. Here's my plan.

## What I'll check, in order

**Step 0 — get raw material I don't have yet.** I need the actual text of all 25 MCQ option sets (A/B/C/D) to do Layer 3 surface analysis (character counts, vocabulary echo, grammatical agreement). The blueprint and style-guide quote selectively. I'll extract option text from `mock-exam.pdf` first.

**Step 1 — tag 25 MCQs across three layers.** Per-question table with:
- L1 *semantic*: defining property | distractor-family | conceptual axis (formula/purpose/failure-mode/when-to-use)
- L2 *logical*: opposites pair? subset/superset? odd-one-out? AOTA/NOTA? negation stem? quantifier traps?
- L3 *surface*: option char counts (longest? most specific?), grammatical fit, vocabulary-echo with stem, position letter

**Step 2 — frequency-count the patterns.** A pattern in ≥3/25 questions becomes a rule. Specific things I'll measure:

- **Position bias** — preliminary count from the answer key already looks suspicious: A=7, B=2, **C=11**, D=5. C at 44% is a strong tell that contradicts the style-guide's "no obvious positional bias" line. I'll verify and treat it as a candidate HIGH-confidence rule unless it dissolves under class-stratification.
- Symmetric-swap distractor frequency (style-guide flagged 8 cases — I'll confirm and check whether the *correct* answer in a swap-pair is the longer/more-specific one)
- Distractor adjacency families: does the prof reliably pull from "the previous lecture concept", "the next concept", or "the opposing technique"?
- Quantifier behaviour: does Millman ("never"/"always" wrong) hold for this prof, or has he defended against it?
- Length bias: is the correct answer reliably the longest? Test against a baseline (random expected = 25%).
- Vocabulary echo: does the correct answer share a key noun with the stem more than distractors?

**Step 3 — class-stratify.** Rerun all frequency counts split by recall / discrimination / computation / application (using exam-hypothesis's classification). Predict where surface tells should fail (discrimination MCQs likely length-balanced; computation MCQs have numerical-clustering tells instead).

**Step 4 — build three artifacts** in `40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/`:
- `mcq-playbook.md` — main artifact. Likely shape: top section is the "30-second sequence" as a ranked decision list; middle is per-pattern rule cards; bottom is class overrides. Confidence labels (HIGH/MED/LOW + count) on every rule.
- `mcq-pattern-evidence.md` — per-question tagging table + frequency tables. Audit trail.
- `mcq-playbook-cheatsheet.md` — one page, 5–7 most reliable moves, ranked.

I'll explicitly **not recapitulate** the three distractor families already in `exam-style-guide.md` (symmetric swap, plausible-but-incomplete, numerical trap) — only extend or refine them. I'll flag the Millman caveat and predict per-pattern brittleness on the real exam.

## Clarifying questions

1. **The position-bias finding (C=11/25=44%) directly contradicts a claim in `exam-style-guide.md`.** Want me to (a) silently override it in the playbook, (b) call it out and update the style guide, or (c) flag it in the playbook only?
2. **Scope of "the prof"** — only this mock, or should I also weight the earlier `mock-1-questions.md` if it has answer keys? My default is **only the official mock** (the calibration sample), since that's what we know is from the prof.
3. **Length expectation** — playbook + evidence + cheatsheet at "thorough but not bloated", or hard cap (e.g. cheatsheet ≤ 1 page printed)?

Awaiting "go".
Reading that as "go". Starting with the option-text extraction.

I have all 25 MCQs with full options. Running the analysis now. Initial findings I want to flag:

- **Position-bias confirmed**: A=7, B=2, **C=11**, D=5. C is at 44% (z≈2.2 vs uniform). The style-guide claim of "no obvious positional bias" was based on visual spread; the actual count is skewed.
- **Opposites/swap structure**: 12/25 questions (48%), and in *every* one the correct answer is in the opposites pair.
- **Absolutism elimination** (perfectly/completely/all/no): 8 distractor instances, 0 correct-answer instances. 8/8 hit rate.
- **Length is weak**: mean correct-option rank 2.38 vs 2.5 baseline; only 52% of correct answers are in the top half by length. Tiebreaker, not a primary rule.
- **Length-outlier rule** (much shorter or much longer than peers): 4 flagged outliers in mock, all 4 wrong (Q11D, Q12D, Q20D, Q22B). Small n but clean.

Writing the three artifacts now.

---
tags: [exam-prep, mcq-patterns, audit-trail, Computer-Vision]
course: Computer-Vision
sources: [mock-exam.pdf, mock-exam-graded.pdf, exam-blueprint.md, exam-style-guide.md, exam-hypothesis.md]
created: 2026-04-28
---

# MCQ Pattern Evidence — Audit Trail

> **Purpose.** This is the data that backs [[mcq-playbook]]. Every rule in the playbook is grounded in a count from the tables below. If a rule fails on the real exam, trace it back here: which questions did it work on? which class? was the count strong enough?
>
> **Scope.** 25 MCQs (Q4–Q28) on `mock-exam.pdf`, with the answer key from `mock-exam-graded.pdf`. **n=25 is small.** Treat any single-question pattern as noise; treat 3+ as signal; treat 8+ as strong signal.

## 1. Per-question 3-layer tagging

Columns: **L1 sem** = (defining property, distractor adjacency-family, conceptual axis). **L2 logic** = (opposites pair? quantifier traps? specificity gap?). **L3 surface** = (correct-option length rank 1–4 with 1=longest; absolutist words flagged in distractors; key tells).

Class codes: **R** = recall, **D** = discrimination, **A** = application, **C** = computation. (Per [[exam-hypothesis|exam-hypothesis Step 2]].)

| Q  | Ans | Class | L1 — distractor family | L1 — axis | L2 — opposites? | L2 — absolutism in distractors | L3 — len rank of correct | L3 — outlier in opt-set? |
|----|-----|-------|------------------------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|------|----|
| 4  | A   | D     | other geometric set relations (subset/superset) | definition | **yes** (zero/perfect overlap) | "Perfect" in B | 2 | — |
| 5  | C   | D     | other CNN architectures (DenseNet/VGG/ViT) | defining-feature | implicit (parallel vs deep stacks) | "all patches", "Global" in D | 4 | — |
| 6  | A   | A     | structure-tensor permutations | application | **yes** (large/small × corner/flat) | "perfectly flat" in C | 3 | — |
| 7  | D   | A     | other spectrum locations | application | **yes** (horizontal-axis vs vertical-axis) | — | 2 | — |
| 8  | C   | D     | other loss families (regression vs classification) | when-to-use | — | — | 1.5 | shortest-outlier B (19) — wrong |
| 9  | A   | R     | other point-cloud algorithms (3D blob, non-rigid) | definition | **yes** (unknown/known) | — | 1 | — |
| 10 | D   | D     | other "matching" properties (complexity, robustness-to-minima) | when-to-use | — | "Linear" in B (overstated) | 3.5 | — |
| 11 | C   | D     | wrong complexity classes | failure-mode/comparison | implicit (faster/slower) | "no asymptotic improvement" in A; "all N" in D | 2 | shortest-outlier D (54) — wrong |
| 12 | A   | D     | other multi-modal paradigms (sup-class, recon, aug) | definition | implicit (similarity directions) | "Maximize" + "via aug" in D (overstated) | 1 | shortest-outlier D (75) — wrong |
| 13 | C   | R     | other filter types (low-pass, 2nd-order, rank) | definition | — | — | 1 | — |
| 14 | C   | D     | other transformer concepts (variable len, depth, batching) | motivation | — | — | 1 | — |
| 15 | A   | R     | invariance × computational-load grid | motivation | **yes** (2×2 grid) | — | 3.5 | — |
| 16 | C   | R     | freq-domain effects (saturation/removal/fold) | failure-mode | **yes** (high→low / low→high) | "completely removed" in B | 1 | — |
| 17 | D   | D     | "connection counts" (output, weights, fan-in) | definition | **yes** (input/output map directionality) | — | 2 | — |
| 18 | C   | A     | parallel/perpendicular × g_y/g_x or g_x/g_y | application/formula | **yes** (2×2 grid) | — | 2 | — |
| 19 | A   | D     | other autograd states (eval mode, weight updates) | definition | **yes** (disables/enables × gradient/weight) | "speeds up training" in B (overstated) | 3 | — |
| 20 | B   | R     | other edge/feature filters (Sobel, Canny, Laplacian) | definition | — | — | 2 | longest-outlier D (49) — wrong |
| 21 | C   | D     | other Canny stages (hysteresis, edge-linking, thicken) | definition | **yes** (thin/thicken in C/D) | — | 4 | — |
| 22 | C   | A     | other shape descriptors | application | **yes** (convex vs concave implicit) | "perfectly convex and smooth" in A | 1.5 | shortest-outlier B (17) — wrong |
| 23 | B   | C     | reduced/increased × 25/50 % | computation | **yes** (2×2 grid) | — | 3.5 | — |
| 24 | C   | C     | broadcasting mistakes (transposed, unchanged, error) | computation | — | — | 3 | longest-outlier A (RuntimeError, 12) — wrong |
| 25 | D   | A     | ≤/≥ × 0.5/2 | application | **yes** (2×2 grid) | — | 4 | — |
| 26 | C   | C     | wrong row of A·V matrix product | computation | — | — | 2.5 (all tied) | — |
| 27 | A   | C     | wrong reduction axis / keepdim variants | computation | — | — | 3.5 | — |
| 28 | D   | C     | broadcasting-error / wrong-shape variants | computation | — | — | 2 | shortest-outlier A (RuntimeError, 12) — wrong |

### Class totals

- **R (Recall)**: Q9, Q13, Q15, Q16, Q20 = **5**
- **D (Discrimination)**: Q4, Q5, Q8, Q10, Q11, Q12, Q14, Q17, Q19, Q21 = **10**
- **A (Application)**: Q6, Q7, Q18, Q22, Q25 = **5**
- **C (Computation)**: Q23, Q24, Q26, Q27, Q28 = **5**

## 2. Layer 3 — Surface frequency tables

### 2.1 Position bias (correct-letter base rate)

| Letter | Count | % | Random null | Z-score* |
|--------|-------|---|-------------|----------|
| A | 7  | 28% | 25% | +0.35 |
| B | 2  | 8%  | 25% | **−1.97** |
| C | 11 | 44% | 25% | **+2.20** |
| D | 5  | 20% | 25% | −0.58 |

*Z under binomial, n=25, p=0.25.

**C is over-represented at p≈0.04**; **B is under-represented at p≈0.05**. Joint position-bias is real on this mock but n=25 is small. **MEDIUM signal**, brittle: probably partly an artifact of the prof's MCQ template (4 options laid out in a 2×2 grid as A C / B D; some answer-key generators default to top-right).

Class-stratified C-rate:
- R: 3/5 = 60% (Q13, Q15(wait no, Q15=A), Q16; let me recount: Q9=A, Q13=C, Q15=A, Q16=C, Q20=B → 2/5 = 40%)
- D: 4/10 = 40% (Q5, Q11, Q14, Q21)
- A: 2/5 = 40% (Q18, Q22)
- C-class: 2/5 = 40% (Q24, Q26)

**The 44% C-rate is uniform across classes** — not driven by any single class.

### 2.2 Length rank of correct option

Mean rank = **2.38** (random null = 2.5). Top-half rate = **13/25 = 52%** (random null = 50%). **Length is a near-null signal — barely above chance.**

Distribution of correct-option length rank:
- Strict longest: 5 (Q9, Q12, Q13, Q14, Q16) = 20%
- Tied longest: 3 (Q8, Q22, Q28) = 12%
- 2nd longest (or rank 2): 7 = 28%
- 3rd–4th: 10 = 40%

**Conclusion: the prof has roughly equalised option lengths.** Length-as-signal is weak. Don't lean on it.

### 2.3 Length-outlier rule (one option markedly different from the other three)

Markedly different = ≥40% length difference from the next-closest peer.

| Q  | Outlier | Direction | Outlier correct? |
|----|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| 11 | D (54)  | shortest  | **No** (correct=C) ✓ rule holds |
| 12 | D (75)  | shortest  | **No** (correct=A) ✓ rule holds |
| 20 | D (49)  | longest   | **No** (correct=B) ✓ rule holds |
| 22 | B (17)  | shortest  | **No** (correct=C) ✓ rule holds |
| 24 | A ("RuntimeError") | longest text | **No** (correct=C) ✓ rule holds |
| 28 | A ("RuntimeError") | shortest text | **No** (correct=D) ✓ rule holds |

**6/6 length outliers are wrong.** This is the strongest surface signal in the mock. **HIGH confidence**, though n=6 is small.

### 2.4 Absolutist / overstatement language elimination

Words flagged: "perfect/perfectly", "complete/completely", "all", "any", "every", "always", "never", "no" (as absolute), "linear" (as exact specification), "Maximize" (when paired with overstated mechanism).

| Q  | Option | Phrase | Correct? |
|----|--------|--------|----------|
| 4  | B | "Perfect overlap" | wrong ✓ |
| 5  | D | "Global self-attention across **all** patches" | wrong ✓ |
| 6  | C | "**perfectly** flat" | wrong ✓ |
| 10 | B | "**Linear** computational complexity" | wrong ✓ |
| 11 | A | "**no** asymptotic improvement" | wrong ✓ |
| 11 | D | "linear scaling for **all** N" | wrong ✓ |
| 12 | D | "**Maximize** similarity ... via data augmentation" | wrong ✓ |
| 16 | B | "**completely** removed" | wrong ✓ |
| 19 | B | "speeds up training" (overstated outcome, not technical hedge) | wrong ✓ |
| 22 | A | "**perfectly** convex and smooth" | wrong ✓ |

**10 absolutist phrases across 8 distractors. Zero in correct answers.** Hit rate 10/10. **HIGH confidence — strongest, most stable surface signal.** Classical Millman rule — and the prof has *not* defended against it.

## 3. Layer 2 — Logical structure frequency tables

### 3.1 Opposites / symmetric-swap structure

Definition: at least two options are exact term-flipped versions of each other (the swap pair). Often the option set is a 2×2 grid of {term-A, term-B} × {term-C, term-D}.

| Q  | Swap structure | Correct in swap pair? |
|----|----------------|------------------------|
| 4  | zero / perfect overlap | yes (A) |
| 6  | large/small × corner/flat | yes (A) |
| 7  | horizontal-axis / vertical-axis spectrum | yes (D) |
| 9  | unknown / known correspondences | yes (A) |
| 11 | faster / slower at O(N log N) | yes (C) |
| 15 | spatial/channel × reduce/increase | yes (A) |
| 16 | high→low / low→high frequency fold | yes (C) |
| 17 | input / output region for receptive field | yes (D) |
| 18 | parallel/perpendicular × g_y/g_x or g_x/g_y | yes (C) |
| 19 | disables/enables × gradient/weight | yes (A) |
| 21 | thin / thicken edges | yes (C) |
| 22 | convex (smooth) / concave (branched) | yes (C, implicit) |
| 23 | reduced/increased × 25/50% | yes (B) |
| 25 | ≤/≥ × 0.5/2 pixels per cm | yes (D) |

**14/25 (56%) questions have explicit swap structure. Correct answer is always inside the swap pair.** **HIGH confidence — most prevalent structural pattern.**

This extends [[exam-style-guide]]'s "(a) symmetric swap" family from 8 examples to 14, and adds the operational rule: **eliminate any non-swap option immediately**.

### 3.2 Quantifier presence in stem

Stems containing "primary", "primarily", "main", "specifically":
- Q9, Q13, Q14, Q15, Q19, Q21 = 6/25 (24%)

These are explicit discrimination signals — the prof is saying "more than one option may be true; pick THE one." Plus stems that use "best defines" (Q17), "key advantage" (Q10), "defining characteristic" (Q5), "main training objective" (Q12), "specifically effective" (Q8).

**Total ~12/25 stems carry a "discrimination cue" word.** When you see one, skip the easy-to-eliminate options and go straight to the technical-specificity comparison.

### 3.3 Negation stems ("which is NOT")

**0 / 25.** The prof does not use negation traps in the stem. (He does invert *meaning* in distractors via symmetric swaps — that's the only inversion trap.)

### 3.4 All-of-the-above / None-of-the-above

**0 / 25.** Not used.

## 4. Layer 1 — Semantic patterns

### 4.1 Distractor adjacency families (cross-question signal)

**Pattern: distractors come from the same lecture or the same concept-cluster.** Cross-lecture distractors are rare.

| Question topic | Distractor source | Cross-lecture? |
|----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Q5 Inception | other CNN architectures (same lecture L17) | no |
| Q8 Dice | other loss families (mostly L15-territory but logical neighbours) | weakly |
| Q9 ICP | other point-cloud algos | no |
| Q10 SIFT | other matching/feature properties | no |
| Q12 CLIP | other multi-modal training paradigms | no |
| Q13 Sobel | other filter types (same lecture L11) | no |
| Q14 MHA | other transformer-concepts | no |
| Q19 no_grad | other autograd states (same lecture L14) | no |
| Q20 Hough | other edge/line filters (same lecture L11–L12) | no |
| Q21 NMS-Canny | other Canny stages | no |

**Implication:** if you don't know the topic, the correct answer **is a property of the topic the stem names**. Eliminate any option that "feels like a different concept's defining property."

This is the operational reframing of [[exam-style-guide]]'s "(b) plausible-but-incomplete": the distractors aren't just plausible — they are **definitively true statements about adjacent concepts**.

### 4.2 Conceptual axis distribution

| Axis | Count | Questions |
|------|-------|-----------|
| Definition / "what is" | 11 | Q4, Q9, Q11, Q12, Q13, Q14, Q15, Q16, Q17, Q19, Q20, Q21 |
| Application / apply rule | 5 | Q6, Q7, Q18, Q22, Q25 |
| Computation | 5 | Q23, Q24, Q26, Q27, Q28 |
| Defining-feature / discrimination | 4 | Q5, Q8, Q10 |
| Comparison / when-to-use | included above | Q8, Q10, Q11 |

Definitional questions dominate (~44%). The prof's preferred test mode is "name the right concept" not "do the math".

## 5. Class-stratified pattern strength

| Pattern | Recall (n=5) | Discrim (n=10) | Application (n=5) | Computation (n=5) |
|---------|--------------|----------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Position-C bias | 2/5 (40%) | 4/10 (40%) | 2/5 (40%) | 2/5 (40%) |
| Has swap structure | 4/5 (80%) | 4/10 (40%) | 5/5 (100%) | 1/5 (20%) |
| Absolutism in distractor | 1/5 (20%) | 6/10 (60%) | 2/5 (40%) | 0/5 (0%) |
| Length-outlier-wrong | 1/5 (Q20) | 3/10 (Q11/Q12/Q22) | 1/5 (Q22) | 2/5 (Q24/Q28) |
| Correct = longest (strict or tied) | 4/5 (80%) | 4/10 (40%) | 1/5 (20%) | 2/5 (40%) |

### Class-specific takeaways

**Recall (n=5):** Length tell *does* work here — 80% of correct answers are at least tied for longest. The "longest = right" rule holds best for pure recall. Swap structure is also high (80%). [Caveat: n=5 — only 4 questions support the length finding.]

**Discrimination (n=10):** The class with most absolutism distractors (60%). The lecture-canonical-vocabulary rule and technical-specificity rule are highest-yield here. Length tell is **near-chance (40%)** — the prof has specifically balanced lengths in the discrimination class to defend against test-wiseness. Swap structure is lower (40%) than the 56% overall — so on a discrimination question, **don't expect a clean swap pair**.

**Application (n=5):** 100% have swap structure (it's how the prof builds rule-application traps — every option is a permutation of the rule's variables). **Surface tells fail** (length tied; absolutism rare). The only way through is to actually apply the rule.

**Computation (n=5):** Surface tells useless. Length-outlier-wrong is the *only* applicable surface rule (helps 2/5 — flag obviously-wrong options like `RuntimeError` when broadcasting clearly works, or vice versa). Otherwise: just compute.

## 6. Per-question confidence sourcing

Each playbook rule's confidence label maps back to:
- Count on this mock (out of 25)
- Class-stratified count
- Whether the prof appears to have *defended* against the rule

| Rule | Mock count | Strongest class | Confidence | Brittleness |
|------|------------|-----------------|------------|-------------|
| Eliminate absolutist options | 10 instances / 25 Qs | Discrimination | **HIGH** | Low — Millman rule, prof shows no defence |
| Pick from swap pair | 14/25 | Application 100% | **HIGH** | Low — structural; prof's signature |
| Distractors = same lecture | qualitative all-Q | all classes | **HIGH** | Low — distractor-design philosophy |
| Eliminate length outlier | 6/6 outliers wrong | Discrimination + Computation | **MEDIUM-HIGH** | Medium — n=6 small, may not generalise |
| C > B as guess letter | 11/25 = 44% C; 2/25 = 8% B | uniform across classes | **MEDIUM** | High — sample-of-25 artifact, prof may rebalance |
| Most technically specific = right | qualitative ~8/10 D class | Discrimination | **MEDIUM-HIGH** | Low for discrim class |
| Lecture-canonical vocab = right | qualitative | Recall + Discrim | **MEDIUM** | Low — assumes you can recognise it |
| Longest = right | 8/25 strict+tied (32%) | Recall (80%) | **LOW overall, MEDIUM for recall** | High — barely above chance |
| Vocabulary echo with stem | not systematically counted | — | **LOW** | High — too noisy to operationalise |
| Odd-one-out | bidirectional (sometimes right, sometimes wrong) | — | **NULL** | — |

## 7. Cross-references

- [[mcq-playbook]] — the rules synthesized from this evidence
- [[mcq-playbook-cheatsheet]] — one-page distillation
- [[exam-style-guide]] — distractor families (a/b/c) that this doc extends
- [[exam-hypothesis]] — class taxonomy (R/D/A/C) used here

---

# MCQ Pattern Evidence — Audit Trail

> **Purpose.** This is the data that backs [[mcq-playbook]]. Every rule in the playbook is grounded in a count from the tables below. If a rule fails on the real exam, trace it back here: which questions did it work on? which class? was the count strong enough?
>
> **Scope.** 25 MCQs (Q4–Q28) on `mock-exam.pdf`, with the answer key from `mock-exam-graded.pdf`. **n=25 is small.** Treat any single-question pattern as noise; treat 3+ as signal; treat 8+ as strong signal.

## 1. Per-question 3-layer tagging

Columns: **L1 sem** = (defining property, distractor adjacency-family, conceptual axis). **L2 logic** = (opposites pair? quantifier traps? specificity gap?). **L3 surface** = (correct-option length rank 1–4 with 1=longest; absolutist words flagged in distractors; key tells).

Class codes: **R** = recall, **D** = discrimination, **A** = application, **C** = computation. (Per [[exam-hypothesis|exam-hypothesis Step 2]].)

| Q  | Ans | Class | L1 — distractor family | L1 — axis | L2 — opposites? | L2 — absolutism in distractors | L3 — len rank of correct | L3 — outlier in opt-set? |
|----|-----|-------|------------------------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|------|----|
| 4  | A   | D     | other geometric set relations (subset/superset) | definition | **yes** (zero/perfect overlap) | "Perfect" in B | 2 | — |
| 5  | C   | D     | other CNN architectures (DenseNet/VGG/ViT) | defining-feature | implicit (parallel vs deep stacks) | "all patches", "Global" in D | 4 | — |
| 6  | A   | A     | structure-tensor permutations | application | **yes** (large/small × corner/flat) | "perfectly flat" in C | 3 | — |
| 7  | D   | A     | other spectrum locations | application | **yes** (horizontal-axis vs vertical-axis) | — | 2 | — |
| 8  | C   | D     | other loss families (regression vs classification) | when-to-use | — | — | 1.5 | shortest-outlier B (19) — wrong |
| 9  | A   | R     | other point-cloud algorithms (3D blob, non-rigid) | definition | **yes** (unknown/known) | — | 1 | — |
| 10 | D   | D     | other "matching" properties (complexity, robustness-to-minima) | when-to-use | — | "Linear" in B (overstated) | 3.5 | — |
| 11 | C   | D     | wrong complexity classes | failure-mode/comparison | implicit (faster/slower) | "no asymptotic improvement" in A; "all N" in D | 2 | shortest-outlier D (54) — wrong |
| 12 | A   | D     | other multi-modal paradigms (sup-class, recon, aug) | definition | implicit (similarity directions) | "Maximize" + "via aug" in D (overstated) | 1 | shortest-outlier D (75) — wrong |
| 13 | C   | R     | other filter types (low-pass, 2nd-order, rank) | definition | — | — | 1 | — |
| 14 | C   | D     | other transformer concepts (variable len, depth, batching) | motivation | — | — | 1 | — |
| 15 | A   | R     | invariance × computational-load grid | motivation | **yes** (2×2 grid) | — | 3.5 | — |
| 16 | C   | R     | freq-domain effects (saturation/removal/fold) | failure-mode | **yes** (high→low / low→high) | "completely removed" in B | 1 | — |
| 17 | D   | D     | "connection counts" (output, weights, fan-in) | definition | **yes** (input/output map directionality) | — | 2 | — |
| 18 | C   | A     | parallel/perpendicular × g_y/g_x or g_x/g_y | application/formula | **yes** (2×2 grid) | — | 2 | — |
| 19 | A   | D     | other autograd states (eval mode, weight updates) | definition | **yes** (disables/enables × gradient/weight) | "speeds up training" in B (overstated) | 3 | — |
| 20 | B   | R     | other edge/feature filters (Sobel, Canny, Laplacian) | definition | — | — | 2 | longest-outlier D (49) — wrong |
| 21 | C   | D     | other Canny stages (hysteresis, edge-linking, thicken) | definition | **yes** (thin/thicken in C/D) | — | 4 | — |
| 22 | C   | A     | other shape descriptors | application | **yes** (convex vs concave implicit) | "perfectly convex and smooth" in A | 1.5 | shortest-outlier B (17) — wrong |
| 23 | B   | C     | reduced/increased × 25/50 % | computation | **yes** (2×2 grid) | — | 3.5 | — |
| 24 | C   | C     | broadcasting mistakes (transposed, unchanged, error) | computation | — | — | 3 | longest-outlier A (RuntimeError, 12) — wrong |
| 25 | D   | A     | ≤/≥ × 0.5/2 | application | **yes** (2×2 grid) | — | 4 | — |
| 26 | C   | C     | wrong row of A·V matrix product | computation | — | — | 2.5 (all tied) | — |
| 27 | A   | C     | wrong reduction axis / keepdim variants | computation | — | — | 3.5 | — |
| 28 | D   | C     | broadcasting-error / wrong-shape variants | computation | — | — | 2 | shortest-outlier A (RuntimeError, 12) — wrong |

### Class totals

- **R (Recall)**: Q9, Q13, Q15, Q16, Q20 = **5**
- **D (Discrimination)**: Q4, Q5, Q8, Q10, Q11, Q12, Q14, Q17, Q19, Q21 = **10**
- **A (Application)**: Q6, Q7, Q18, Q22, Q25 = **5**
- **C (Computation)**: Q23, Q24, Q26, Q27, Q28 = **5**

## 2. Layer 3 — Surface frequency tables

### 2.1 Position bias (correct-letter base rate)

| Letter | Count | % | Random null | Z-score* |
|--------|-------|---|-------------|----------|
| A | 7  | 28% | 25% | +0.35 |
| B | 2  | 8%  | 25% | **−1.97** |
| C | 11 | 44% | 25% | **+2.20** |
| D | 5  | 20% | 25% | −0.58 |

*Z under binomial, n=25, p=0.25.

**C is over-represented at p≈0.04**; **B is under-represented at p≈0.05**. Joint position-bias is real on this mock but n=25 is small. **MEDIUM signal**, brittle: probably partly an artifact of the prof's MCQ template (4 options laid out in a 2×2 grid as A C / B D; some answer-key generators default to top-right).

Class-stratified C-rate:
- R: 3/5 = 60% (Q13, Q15(wait no, Q15=A), Q16; let me recount: Q9=A, Q13=C, Q15=A, Q16=C, Q20=B → 2/5 = 40%)
- D: 4/10 = 40% (Q5, Q11, Q14, Q21)
- A: 2/5 = 40% (Q18, Q22)
- C-class: 2/5 = 40% (Q24, Q26)

**The 44% C-rate is uniform across classes** — not driven by any single class.

### 2.2 Length rank of correct option

Mean rank = **2.38** (random null = 2.5). Top-half rate = **13/25 = 52%** (random null = 50%). **Length is a near-null signal — barely above chance.**

Distribution of correct-option length rank:
- Strict longest: 5 (Q9, Q12, Q13, Q14, Q16) = 20%
- Tied longest: 3 (Q8, Q22, Q28) = 12%
- 2nd longest (or rank 2): 7 = 28%
- 3rd–4th: 10 = 40%

**Conclusion: the prof has roughly equalised option lengths.** Length-as-signal is weak. Don't lean on it.

### 2.3 Length-outlier rule (one option markedly different from the other three)

Markedly different = ≥40% length difference from the next-closest peer.

| Q  | Outlier | Direction | Outlier correct? |
|----|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| 11 | D (54)  | shortest  | **No** (correct=C) ✓ rule holds |
| 12 | D (75)  | shortest  | **No** (correct=A) ✓ rule holds |
| 20 | D (49)  | longest   | **No** (correct=B) ✓ rule holds |
| 22 | B (17)  | shortest  | **No** (correct=C) ✓ rule holds |
| 24 | A ("RuntimeError") | longest text | **No** (correct=C) ✓ rule holds |
| 28 | A ("RuntimeError") | shortest text | **No** (correct=D) ✓ rule holds |

**6/6 length outliers are wrong.** This is the strongest surface signal in the mock. **HIGH confidence**, though n=6 is small.

### 2.4 Absolutist / overstatement language elimination

Words flagged: "perfect/perfectly", "complete/completely", "all", "any", "every", "always", "never", "no" (as absolute), "linear" (as exact specification), "Maximize" (when paired with overstated mechanism).

| Q  | Option | Phrase | Correct? |
|----|--------|--------|----------|
| 4  | B | "Perfect overlap" | wrong ✓ |
| 5  | D | "Global self-attention across **all** patches" | wrong ✓ |
| 6  | C | "**perfectly** flat" | wrong ✓ |
| 10 | B | "**Linear** computational complexity" | wrong ✓ |
| 11 | A | "**no** asymptotic improvement" | wrong ✓ |
| 11 | D | "linear scaling for **all** N" | wrong ✓ |
| 12 | D | "**Maximize** similarity ... via data augmentation" | wrong ✓ |
| 16 | B | "**completely** removed" | wrong ✓ |
| 19 | B | "speeds up training" (overstated outcome, not technical hedge) | wrong ✓ |
| 22 | A | "**perfectly** convex and smooth" | wrong ✓ |

**10 absolutist phrases across 8 distractors. Zero in correct answers.** Hit rate 10/10. **HIGH confidence — strongest, most stable surface signal.** Classical Millman rule — and the prof has *not* defended against it.

## 3. Layer 2 — Logical structure frequency tables

### 3.1 Opposites / symmetric-swap structure

Definition: at least two options are exact term-flipped versions of each other (the swap pair). Often the option set is a 2×2 grid of {term-A, term-B} × {term-C, term-D}.

| Q  | Swap structure | Correct in swap pair? |
|----|----------------|------------------------|
| 4  | zero / perfect overlap | yes (A) |
| 6  | large/small × corner/flat | yes (A) |
| 7  | horizontal-axis / vertical-axis spectrum | yes (D) |
| 9  | unknown / known correspondences | yes (A) |
| 11 | faster / slower at O(N log N) | yes (C) |
| 15 | spatial/channel × reduce/increase | yes (A) |
| 16 | high→low / low→high frequency fold | yes (C) |
| 17 | input / output region for receptive field | yes (D) |
| 18 | parallel/perpendicular × g_y/g_x or g_x/g_y | yes (C) |
| 19 | disables/enables × gradient/weight | yes (A) |
| 21 | thin / thicken edges | yes (C) |
| 22 | convex (smooth) / concave (branched) | yes (C, implicit) |
| 23 | reduced/increased × 25/50% | yes (B) |
| 25 | ≤/≥ × 0.5/2 pixels per cm | yes (D) |

**14/25 (56%) questions have explicit swap structure. Correct answer is always inside the swap pair.** **HIGH confidence — most prevalent structural pattern.**

This extends [[exam-style-guide]]'s "(a) symmetric swap" family from 8 examples to 14, and adds the operational rule: **eliminate any non-swap option immediately**.

### 3.2 Quantifier presence in stem

Stems containing "primary", "primarily", "main", "specifically":
- Q9, Q13, Q14, Q15, Q19, Q21 = 6/25 (24%)

These are explicit discrimination signals — the prof is saying "more than one option may be true; pick THE one." Plus stems that use "best defines" (Q17), "key advantage" (Q10), "defining characteristic" (Q5), "main training objective" (Q12), "specifically effective" (Q8).

**Total ~12/25 stems carry a "discrimination cue" word.** When you see one, skip the easy-to-eliminate options and go straight to the technical-specificity comparison.

### 3.3 Negation stems ("which is NOT")

**0 / 25.** The prof does not use negation traps in the stem. (He does invert *meaning* in distractors via symmetric swaps — that's the only inversion trap.)

### 3.4 All-of-the-above / None-of-the-above

**0 / 25.** Not used.

## 4. Layer 1 — Semantic patterns

### 4.1 Distractor adjacency families (cross-question signal)

**Pattern: distractors come from the same lecture or the same concept-cluster.** Cross-lecture distractors are rare.

| Question topic | Distractor source | Cross-lecture? |
|----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Q5 Inception | other CNN architectures (same lecture L17) | no |
| Q8 Dice | other loss families (mostly L15-territory but logical neighbours) | weakly |
| Q9 ICP | other point-cloud algos | no |
| Q10 SIFT | other matching/feature properties | no |
| Q12 CLIP | other multi-modal training paradigms | no |
| Q13 Sobel | other filter types (same lecture L11) | no |
| Q14 MHA | other transformer-concepts | no |
| Q19 no_grad | other autograd states (same lecture L14) | no |
| Q20 Hough | other edge/line filters (same lecture L11–L12) | no |
| Q21 NMS-Canny | other Canny stages | no |

**Implication:** if you don't know the topic, the correct answer **is a property of the topic the stem names**. Eliminate any option that "feels like a different concept's defining property."

This is the operational reframing of [[exam-style-guide]]'s "(b) plausible-but-incomplete": the distractors aren't just plausible — they are **definitively true statements about adjacent concepts**.

### 4.2 Conceptual axis distribution

| Axis | Count | Questions |
|------|-------|-----------|
| Definition / "what is" | 11 | Q4, Q9, Q11, Q12, Q13, Q14, Q15, Q16, Q17, Q19, Q20, Q21 |
| Application / apply rule | 5 | Q6, Q7, Q18, Q22, Q25 |
| Computation | 5 | Q23, Q24, Q26, Q27, Q28 |
| Defining-feature / discrimination | 4 | Q5, Q8, Q10 |
| Comparison / when-to-use | included above | Q8, Q10, Q11 |

Definitional questions dominate (~44%). The prof's preferred test mode is "name the right concept" not "do the math".

## 5. Class-stratified pattern strength

| Pattern | Recall (n=5) | Discrim (n=10) | Application (n=5) | Computation (n=5) |
|---------|--------------|----------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Position-C bias | 2/5 (40%) | 4/10 (40%) | 2/5 (40%) | 2/5 (40%) |
| Has swap structure | 4/5 (80%) | 4/10 (40%) | 5/5 (100%) | 1/5 (20%) |
| Absolutism in distractor | 1/5 (20%) | 6/10 (60%) | 2/5 (40%) | 0/5 (0%) |
| Length-outlier-wrong | 1/5 (Q20) | 3/10 (Q11/Q12/Q22) | 1/5 (Q22) | 2/5 (Q24/Q28) |
| Correct = longest (strict or tied) | 4/5 (80%) | 4/10 (40%) | 1/5 (20%) | 2/5 (40%) |

### Class-specific takeaways

**Recall (n=5):** Length tell *does* work here — 80% of correct answers are at least tied for longest. The "longest = right" rule holds best for pure recall. Swap structure is also high (80%). [Caveat: n=5 — only 4 questions support the length finding.]

**Discrimination (n=10):** The class with most absolutism distractors (60%). The lecture-canonical-vocabulary rule and technical-specificity rule are highest-yield here. Length tell is **near-chance (40%)** — the prof has specifically balanced lengths in the discrimination class to defend against test-wiseness. Swap structure is lower (40%) than the 56% overall — so on a discrimination question, **don't expect a clean swap pair**.

**Application (n=5):** 100% have swap structure (it's how the prof builds rule-application traps — every option is a permutation of the rule's variables). **Surface tells fail** (length tied; absolutism rare). The only way through is to actually apply the rule.

**Computation (n=5):** Surface tells useless. Length-outlier-wrong is the *only* applicable surface rule (helps 2/5 — flag obviously-wrong options like `RuntimeError` when broadcasting clearly works, or vice versa). Otherwise: just compute.

## 6. Per-question confidence sourcing

Each playbook rule's confidence label maps back to:
- Count on this mock (out of 25)
- Class-stratified count
- Whether the prof appears to have *defended* against the rule

| Rule | Mock count | Strongest class | Confidence | Brittleness |
|------|------------|-----------------|------------|-------------|
| Eliminate absolutist options | 10 instances / 25 Qs | Discrimination | **HIGH** | Low — Millman rule, prof shows no defence |
| Pick from swap pair | 14/25 | Application 100% | **HIGH** | Low — structural; prof's signature |
| Distractors = same lecture | qualitative all-Q | all classes | **HIGH** | Low — distractor-design philosophy |
| Eliminate length outlier | 6/6 outliers wrong | Discrimination + Computation | **MEDIUM-HIGH** | Medium — n=6 small, may not generalise |
| C > B as guess letter | 11/25 = 44% C; 2/25 = 8% B | uniform across classes | **MEDIUM** | High — sample-of-25 artifact, prof may rebalance |
| Most technically specific = right | qualitative ~8/10 D class | Discrimination | **MEDIUM-HIGH** | Low for discrim class |
| Lecture-canonical vocab = right | qualitative | Recall + Discrim | **MEDIUM** | Low — assumes you can recognise it |
| Longest = right | 8/25 strict+tied (32%) | Recall (80%) | **LOW overall, MEDIUM for recall** | High — barely above chance |
| Vocabulary echo with stem | not systematically counted | — | **LOW** | High — too noisy to operationalise |
| Odd-one-out | bidirectional (sometimes right, sometimes wrong) | — | **NULL** | — |

## 7. Cross-references

- [[mcq-playbook]] — the rules synthesized from this evidence
- [[mcq-playbook-cheatsheet]] — one-page distillation
- [[exam-style-guide]] — distractor families (a/b/c) that this doc extends
- [[exam-hypothesis]] — class taxonomy (R/D/A/C) used here
- Source PDFs: [[../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/mock-exams/mock-exam.pdf]], [[../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/mock-exams/mock-exam-graded.pdf]]

---
tags: [exam-prep, mcq-playbook, Computer-Vision]
course: Computer-Vision
exam-date: 2026-04-29
created: 2026-04-28
---

# MCQ "No-Clue" Playbook

> **Read this first.** This document is for the moments on the real exam when you genuinely don't know the answer. It is **not a study replacement**. Pattern-spotting on this prof's MCQ design buys you ~1–2 marks of expected value across 25 questions (≈ 0.3–0.6 pts) — the other 7 marks of the MCQ block come from knowing the material. **Use this AFTER you've eliminated everything you actually know.**
>
> **Source.** All rules grounded in the 25 MCQs of `mock-exam.pdf` + answer key from `mock-exam-graded.pdf`. Frequency counts and audit trail in [[mcq-pattern-evidence]]. Distractor families (symmetric swap, plausible-but-incomplete, numerical trap) named in [[exam-style-guide]] are the *foundation*; the rules below extend them with operational counts.
>
> **Confidence labels** on every rule:
> - **HIGH** — pattern in 8+ questions OR 100% hit rate on the mock with n≥5.
> - **MEDIUM** — pattern in 3–7 questions, or HIGH but at risk of anti-test-wiseness defence.
> - **LOW** — pattern present but weak / inconsistent. Tiebreaker only.

---

## 1. The 30-second "I have no idea" sequence

Run this top-to-bottom on any MCQ where you've drawn a blank. Stop as soon as a step gives you a clear answer.

| # | Move | Why | Stop here if... |
|---|------|-----|-----------------|
| 1 | **Read the stem twice. Look for "primary", "main", "specifically", "best defines", "key" — these flag a discrimination question.** | 12/25 stems carry a discrimination cue. The presence of one tells you "more than one option is true; pick THE defining one". Skip the rest of this sequence and go to §3 Rule D1. | Stem has a discrimination cue → §3 D1 |
| 2 | **Eliminate any option containing "perfectly", "completely", "always", "never", "all", "no [improvement/effect]", "linear in N" used as an exact specification.** | 10/25 distractors carry absolutist language; **0/25 correct answers do**. This is the single most reliable surface rule. | After elimination, only one option left → done. Otherwise continue. |
| 3 | **Look for a swap pair (two options that are exact term-flips of each other, often in a 2×2 grid).** Eliminate any option NOT in the swap pair. | 14/25 questions have swap structure; correct answer is *always* in the swap pair. Non-swap options are filler designed to look plausible. | Now have 2 options instead of 4 → coin-flip moves to ≥50%. Use §3 R3 specificity tiebreaker. |
| 4 | **Eliminate the length outlier.** If one option is markedly shorter or longer than the other three (≥40% length difference), eliminate it. | 6/6 length-outliers were wrong on the mock. Small n, but clean. | Continue. |
| 5 | **Apply the adjacency rule.** The stem names a topic X. Of the remaining options, eliminate any that "feels like the defining property of a different concept." Distractors come from the SAME LECTURE family (DenseNet/VGG/ViT for an Inception question; MSE/Huber/CE for a Dice question). The correct option is the one whose phrasing most directly anchors to X. | The prof's distractor design philosophy. Rarely cross-lecture. | Continue. |
| 6 | **If two options remain and you can't decide, pick the technically more specific one.** "Different representation subspaces" beats "process variable length"; "Dice (or IoU-based)" beats "Standard Cross-Entropy"; "Perpendicular ... arctan(g_y/g_x) ± 90°" beats "Parallel ... arctan(g_y/g_x)". | The prof's correct-answer style: the *most precise* phrasing wins. Keyword-matchers lose; technical specificity wins. | Decide. |
| 7 | **If still genuinely tied, guess C, then A, then D, then B.** Position bias on this mock: C=44%, A=28%, D=20%, B=8%. **Use this only as last-resort tiebreaker** — it's the most brittle rule (sample of 25). | Last resort. | Mark C and move on. |

**Time budget:** steps 1–7 should take 30–45 seconds total. If they take longer, you've spent more time gaming the question than just thinking about the topic — go back to step 1 and try to recall.

---

## 2. Class-specific overrides

The 25 MCQs split into four cognitive classes (per [[exam-hypothesis]] Step 2). Some rules behave differently per class. **Identify the class first** — then apply the override.

### How to recognise the class quickly

- **Computation (5/25 ≈ 20%)**: Stem contains tensor shapes, code, a matrix, or "what is the output of...". → §2.1
- **Discrimination (10/25 ≈ 40%)**: Stem has "primary", "main", "best defines", "key advantage", "defining characteristic", or "specifically effective". → §2.2
- **Application (5/25 ≈ 20%)**: Stem describes a scenario and asks you to apply a rule ("If an image contains...", "Given the eigenvalues...", "If sampling rate is too low..."). → §2.3
- **Recall (5/25 ≈ 20%)**: Stem says "what is the primary function of X" or "which algorithm does Y" with no scenario. → §2.4

### 2.1 Computation override

> **Surface tells are USELESS here. Just compute.**

- Of the 5 computation MCQs, the only surface signal is **length-outlier-wrong** (Q24 and Q28 both have `RuntimeError` as a length-outlier; both wrong).
- Position-C bias still holds at the same rate (40%) but is not a substitute for actually computing.
- **Distractor families to expect (anchor what you compute against):**
  - **Off-by-one or factor-of-2** (Q23: 25% vs 50%)
  - **Transposed shape** (Q24: (8,4,16) vs (4,8,16))
  - **Input-unchanged** (Q24, Q27 both include the un-broadcast input shape as a trap)
  - **Wrong row/column** of the matrix product (Q26)
  - **`RuntimeError` as a trap** when broadcasting actually works, or as a trap-by-omission when it doesn't.
- **If you cannot compute and time is up:** eliminate `RuntimeError` if shapes look broadcastable; eliminate the input-unchanged shape; pick from what remains. Don't trust position bias here.

### 2.2 Discrimination override

> **Highest-leverage class. The 30-second sequence works best here. Length tell does NOT — the prof has balanced option lengths in this class (40% top-half rate, indistinguishable from chance).**

- Stems with discrimination cues ("primary", "main", "specifically") are explicit signals: **all options state things that are at least partially true**. The trap is grabbing the first option that "sounds right" — it's true, but adjacent.
- Apply rules in this order: **(1) absolutism elimination** (60% of D-class questions have an absolutist distractor), **(2) lecture-canonical-vocabulary**, **(3) technical-specificity tiebreaker**.
- **Skip step 4 (length outlier)** — discrimination distractors are mostly length-balanced, with the exception of Q11/Q12 where a too-short option is wrong.
- The "most precise wording" rule is at its strongest here. From the mock: "parallel convolutional branches" (Q5), "Dice Loss (or IoU-based)" (Q8), "different representation subspaces" (Q14), "input image that can influence that neuron's activation" (Q17), "Disables gradient tracking and reduces memory usage" (Q19) — all are the technically most specific phrasings in their option set.

### 2.3 Application override

> **5/5 application questions have swap-pair structure. So step 3 of the sequence is HIGHEST-leverage here, not step 2. There are essentially no absolutism distractors (only Q22 has one).**

- The prof builds application questions as a 2×2 grid of *rule-permutations*. Q15: {spatial, channel} × {reduce, increase}. Q18: {parallel, perpendicular} × {g_y/g_x, g_x/g_y}. Q23: {reduced, increased} × {25%, 50%}. Q25: {≤, ≥} × {0.5, 2}. Q6 and Q7 are 4-option permutations of structure-tensor or stripe-orientation rules.
- **You cannot eliminate via swap pair alone** — both members of the swap pair encode opposing applications of the rule. You need to pick the *correct* application.
- **You CAN eliminate the non-permutation option** if one exists (in Q4 the IoU question, the subset/superset options are non-permutation distractors and can be dropped first).
- **Surface tells fail** (length-balanced; rare absolutism). Last-resort guess: **position-C bias at 40%**.
- **The hard truth:** if you genuinely don't know how to apply the rule, your best move is a coin-flip between the two swap-pair members. The mock provides no surface signal that breaks the tie. Don't waste time gaming an application question — guess and move on.

### 2.4 Recall override

> **Length tell DOES work here**: 4/5 correct answers are tied or strictly longest in their option set. (Caveat: n=5.) Plus swap structure is high (80% — Q15, Q16, Q20 had swaps; Q9 had unknown/known; Q13 was the outlier with no swap).

- For recall MCQs, the order shifts: **(1) absolutism elim** (one case, Q16 "completely removed"), **(2) length tell** (longest = right, 4/5), **(3) swap pair**, **(4) lecture-canonical-vocabulary**.

**Rule.** Any option containing **"perfectly", "completely", "always", "never", "all" (universal), "no" (as in "no improvement"), "linear" (as exact spec), "Maximize" + overstated mechanism** is wrong.

**Mock evidence.** 10 distractor instances flagged across Q4, Q5, Q6, Q10, Q11(×2), Q12, Q16, Q19, Q22. **Zero in correct answers.**

**Strongest class.** Discrimination (60% of D-class has an absolutism distractor).

**Brittleness on real exam: LOW.** This is classical Millman. The prof has not defended against it on the mock — every absolutist distractor is wrong. The mechanism (correct answer = calibrated, distractor = overstated) is consistent with how the prof writes correct answers (precise, hedged with mechanism). **Trust this rule.**

**Caveat.** Don't eliminate technical absolutes that are *true*: "**Linear** classification head" describing softmax, "**O(N log N)** scaling" — these are technical descriptors, not overstatements. The signal is overstatement of an effect or universality of a property, not the presence of the word.

---

### A2 — Pick from the swap pair, eliminate filler [HIGH]

**Rule.** If two of the four options are exact term-flips of each other (or four options form a 2×2 permutation grid), the correct answer is in the swap pair. Eliminate any option not in the swap pair.

**Mock evidence.** 14/25 questions have swap structure. Correct answer in swap pair: **14/14 = 100%.**

**Strongest class.** Application (5/5 have swap structure). Also high in Recall (4/5).

**When the rule eliminates 2/4:** Q4 (subset/superset options not part of swap), Q9 (blob/non-rigid options not part of swap), Q11 (O(N²) and O(N) not in the O(N log N) swap pair), Q17 (weights/connections not part of input/output map swap), Q21 (low-gradient/connect not part of thin/thicken swap). **Brittleness on real exam: LOW.** This is the prof's distractor-construction philosophy. He doesn't pull tricks from unrelated lectures. The real exam will follow the same pattern.

**Operational use.** When you don't know X but recognise that an option describes Y (a different concept), eliminate that option. The right answer must be a property of X.

**Edge case.** Q8 distractors include MSE (regression) — that's L15, not L18. Cross-lecture distractor exists, but it's logically adjacent (the loss-function family). ---

**Rule.** If one option is markedly different in length from the other three (≥40% length gap), it's wrong. Whether it's the longest or the shortest doesn't matter.

**Strongest classes.** Discrimination + Computation.

When a distractor is conspicuously short, it's usually a "did you blank-fill the keyword without checking?" trap (Q11D, Q22B). When it's conspicuously long, it's usually a "throw in extra plausible-sounding modifiers" trap (Q20D).

---

### B2 — Most technically specific = right [MEDIUM-HIGH]

**Rule.** Among the remaining options, the one with the most precise technical phrasing — most lecture-specific vocabulary, most hedged with the actual mechanism, least vague — is the answer.

**Mock evidence.** Qualitative ~8/10 D-class questions follow this. **Strongest class.** Discrimination.

**Brittleness on real exam: LOW for D-class.** The prof's correct-answer style is consistent across the mock — precision wins. **Higher risk on Recall** if the right answer happens to be a single short term (Q20: "The Hough Transform" is just a name, not a precise phrase).

**Operational use.** When two options seem equally correct, ask: "which one would I quote in a textbook?" That's the answer.

---

**Mock evidence.** Position-letter base rate, n=25.

---

- **"Longest option = right" (the classic Millman length rule).** Mean correct-option rank 2.38 vs 2.5 baseline. Top-half rate 52% vs 50%. **Near-null signal.** Only useful in the Recall class (4/5 = 80%) — and even there, n=5 is too small to fully trust. **Demoted to Recall-class tiebreaker only.**
- **Vocabulary echo (correct answer reuses stem nouns).** Couldn't operationalise reliably; too noisy. Real but not extractable into a rule.
- **Odd-one-out.** Bidirectional: sometimes the odd option is right (Q5 — "parallel branches" is the only non-architecture-name option), sometimes wrong (Q11 — O(N) is the only non-O(N²)/O(N log N) option, and it's wrong). No usable rule.
- **Subset/superset.** Rare structure on this mock; not a rule.
- **All-of-the-above / None-of-the-above.** **0 instances.** Don't waste mental cycles looking for these.
- **Negation stems ("which is NOT...").** **0 instances.** The prof doesn't use these. **Don't expect them on the real exam.**

---

Assume any of the following on the real exam, and the playbook degrades:

1. **The prof rebalances option positions.** C-bias dies; §1 step 7 becomes a coin flip. Loss: maybe 0.3 pts on questions you're guessing blind.
2. **The prof equalises option lengths in all classes (not just discrimination).** B1 length-outlier rule loses 1–2 marks of expected value. Still leaves A1, A2, A3 intact.
3. **The prof adds a "which is NOT" question.** Negation traps require flipped logic — re-read the stem twice before applying any rule.
4. **The real exam tests L15 / L20 topics** ([[exam-hypothesis|hypothesis]] predicts this). ---

## 6. Decision tree (compressed)

```
Have I eliminated everything I actually know?
├── No → keep eliminating from knowledge before using rules.
└── Yes → enter playbook.
        │
        ├── Stem cue ("primary"/"main"/"best defines")?
        │   └── Yes → Discrimination override (§2.2):
        │           A1 (absolutism) → B3 (lecture vocab) → B2 (specificity).
        │
        ├── Stem has tensor shapes / matrix / code?
        │   └── Yes → Computation override (§2.1):
        │           B1 only useful surface tell. Else compute or eliminate
        │           "RuntimeError" / "input-unchanged" traps and pick.
        │
        ├── Stem describes a scenario ("if/given/when")?
        │   └── Yes → Application override (§2.3):
        │           A2 to drop non-permutation options, then coin-flip
        │           inside the swap pair. C1 if forced.
        │
        └── Recall stem ("what is the function of X")?
            └── Yes → Recall override (§2.4):
                    A1 → length tell (longest = right) → A2 → B3.
```

---

- **Computation (5/25 ≈ 20%)**: Stem contains tensor shapes, code, a matrix, or "what is the output of...". → §2.1

> **Surface tells are USELESS here. Just compute.**

**Brittleness on real exam: LOW for in-vocab cases, but MEDIUM as an exam strategy** — assumes you can recognise lecture vocab on sight. Worthless if you don't.

**Operational use.** This is the rule that benefits most from review. Drill the [[exam-style-guide]] §5 vocabulary table; on the exam, the option containing one of those terms is usually the answer.

---

### C1 — Position-C bias [MEDIUM, HIGHEST BRITTLENESS]

**Rule.** When forced to guess randomly with no other tell: **C > A > D > B**. (44% / 28% / 20% / 8% on the mock.)

**Mock evidence.** Position-letter base rate, n=25.

**Brittleness on real exam: HIGH.** Sample of 25 is small. C-rate of 44% is statistically marginal (z ≈ +2.2). The prof may have written the real exam with rebalanced positions. **Use ONLY as a last-resort tiebreaker** — never override another rule with this.

**Hypothesis for why C exists.** The MCQ template lays options in a 2×2 grid (A C / B D); some answer-key construction methods bias toward top-right. If true, the bias would persist into the real exam. If random, it won't. **Cannot tell from a single mock.**

**Operational use.** If you've eliminated to one option, mark it. If you've eliminated to two and they're a swap pair you can't break, prefer C over A over D over B *only if it's still a coin-flip*.

---

### C2 — "Primary" / "main" stem cue → discrimination class [MEDIUM-HIGH meta-rule]

**Rule.** When the stem contains "primary", "primarily", "main", "specifically", "best defines", "key advantage", "defining characteristic": expect 3 plausible-but-incomplete distractors. Skip surface tells. Apply A1, B2, B3 in that order.

**Mock evidence.** 12/25 stems have a discrimination cue; correlation with D-class is high.

**Brittleness on real exam: LOW** — this is a meta-rule about *which other rules to use*, not a rule that itself can be wrong.

---

## 4. What this playbook does NOT include (rules tested and rejected)

For transparency. Rules that *seemed* like signals but didn't survive the count.

- **"Longest option = right" (the classic Millman length rule).** Mean correct-option rank 2.38 vs 2.5 baseline. Top-half rate 52% vs 50%. **Near-null signal.** Only useful in the Recall class (4/5 = 80%) — and even there, n=5 is too small to fully trust. **Demoted to Recall-class tiebreaker only.**
- **Vocabulary echo (correct answer reuses stem nouns).** Couldn't operationalise reliably; too noisy. Real but not extractable into a rule.
- **Odd-one-out.** Bidirectional: sometimes the odd option is right (Q5 — "parallel branches" is the only non-architecture-name option), sometimes wrong (Q11 — O(N) is the only non-O(N²)/O(N log N) option, and it's wrong). No usable rule.
- **Subset/superset.** Rare structure on this mock; not a rule.
- **All-of-the-above / None-of-the-above.** **0 instances.** Don't waste mental cycles looking for these.
- **Negation stems ("which is NOT...").** **0 instances.** The prof doesn't use these. **Don't expect them on the real exam.**

---

## 5. The honesty section: where this playbook will fail

Assume any of the following on the real exam, and the playbook degrades:

1. **The prof rebalances option positions.** C-bias dies; §1 step 7 becomes a coin flip. Loss: maybe 0.3 pts on questions you're guessing blind.
2. **The prof equalises option lengths in all classes (not just discrimination).** B1 length-outlier rule loses 1–2 marks of expected value. Still leaves A1, A2, A3 intact.
3. **The prof adds a "which is NOT" question.** Negation traps require flipped logic — re-read the stem twice before applying any rule.
4. **The real exam tests L15 / L20 topics** ([[exam-hypothesis|hypothesis]] predicts this). Distractor families on those topics are *unknown* — you have no mock precedent for what swap pairs the prof builds in cross-entropy / VAE / ResNet questions. **Apply A3 (adjacency) and A1 (absolutism) confidently; treat B1, B2, B3 with caution; don't trust C1 at all.**

**Rule of thumb:** the more familiar the topic territory (Fourier, transformers, CNNs basics, PyTorch broadcasting), the more the surface playbook holds. The further from mock territory the question feels (L15 losses, L20 autoencoders, ResNet), the more you should trust your knowledge over surface cues. **The playbook is a parachute, not a lifeboat.**

---

## 6. Decision tree (compressed)

```
Have I eliminated everything I actually know?
├── No → keep eliminating from knowledge before using rules.
└── Yes → enter playbook.
        │
        ├── Stem cue ("primary"/"main"/"best defines")?
        │   └── Yes → Discrimination override (§2.2):
        │           A1 (absolutism) → B3 (lecture vocab) → B2 (specificity).
        │
        ├── Stem has tensor shapes / matrix / code?
        │   └── Yes → Computation override (§2.1):
        │           B1 only useful surface tell. Else compute or eliminate
        │           "RuntimeError" / "input-unchanged" traps and pick.
        │
        ├── Stem describes a scenario ("if/given/when")?
        │   └── Yes → Application override (§2.3):
        │           A2 to drop non-permutation options, then coin-flip
        │           inside the swap pair. C1 if forced.
        │
        └── Recall stem ("what is the function of X")?
            └── Yes → Recall override (§2.4):
                    A1 → length tell (longest = right) → A2 → B3.
```

---

## 7. Cross-references

- [[mcq-pattern-evidence]] — the count tables backing every rule above
- [[mcq-playbook-cheatsheet]] — one-page version for the exam itself
- [[exam-style-guide]] — distractor families that ground the rules; vocabulary list for B3
- [[exam-hypothesis]] — class taxonomy (R/D/A/C); predicted real-exam topics
- [[exam-blueprint]] — per-question topic/difficulty mapping

# MCQ Cheatsheet — Scan This During the Exam

> **Use only after you've eliminated everything you actually know.** Pattern-spotting buys ~1–2 marks; knowledge buys 7. See [[mcq-playbook]] for full rules and brittleness notes.

## The 5 moves, ranked

**1. Eliminate absolutist words.** [HIGH, 10/10 hit]
   *"perfectly", "completely", "always", "never", "all" (universal), "no" (as in "no improvement"), "Maximize... via X" with overstated mechanism.*
   Zero correct answers on the mock used these words. Ten distractors did.

**2. Find the swap pair, eliminate filler.** [HIGH, 14/14 hit]
   When two options are exact term-flips of each other (zero/perfect, unknown/known, parallel/perpendicular, reduced/increased, ≤/≥), the answer is in that pair. Drop everything outside it.
   *Caveat: when all four options form a 2×2 permutation grid (Q15, Q18, Q23, Q25 territory), this eliminates nothing — apply the rule.*

**3. Adjacency check.** [HIGH]
   Distractors come from the same lecture as the stem topic. They describe **adjacent concepts**: Inception's distractors are DenseNet/VGG/ViT; Dice's are MSE/Huber/CE. **If an option's content is the defining property of a different concept you recognise, eliminate it.**

**4. Length outlier is wrong.** [MEDIUM-HIGH, 6/6 hit]
   If one option is markedly shorter or longer than the other three, eliminate it. (Includes `RuntimeError` outliers in code questions.)

**5. Most technically specific wins.** [MEDIUM-HIGH for discrimination class]
   Of the remaining options, pick the one with the most precise lecture-vocab phrasing. Vague verbs lose; precise mechanism wins. "Different representation subspaces" beats "process variable length"; "input image that can influence that neuron's activation" beats anything looser.

**Last-resort tiebreaker:** position bias on the mock was C=44%, A=28%, D=20%, B=8%. If two options remain and you have no other tell, pick **C**. Never override another rule with this — it's the most brittle pattern.

## Class quick-reference

| Stem signal | Class | Best rules (in order) | Avoid |
|-------------|-------|------------------------|-------|
| "primary", "main", "best defines", "key", "specifically" | **Discrimination** (40%) | 1 → 5 → 3 (specificity is king) | Length tell (balanced in this class) |
| Tensor shapes / code / matrix in stem | **Computation** (20%) | Just compute. Else: 4 (drop `RuntimeError` outliers and "input-unchanged" shape traps) | All other surface tells |
| "If / given / when" scenario | **Application** (20%) | 2 (then coin-flip inside swap pair) | All non-knowledge tells |
| "What is the function of X" | **Recall** (20%) | 1 → 4 → length-rule (longest = right, 4/5 in this class) | Specificity (right answer might be a bare term) |

## Stems that DON'T appear on this prof's exams

- **No "which is NOT" / negation stems.** 0/25 on the mock.
- **No "all of the above" / "none of the above".** 0/25 on the mock.
- **No tricks in the stem itself** — the prof actively pre-empts misreadings (e.g. Q18 includes `(the direction along the edge itself)` to prevent gradient-vs-edge confusion). The traps are in the distractor design only.

## What to mistrust on the real exam

- **C-position bias is the most brittle rule.** Sample of 25; could vanish on a re-balanced real exam.
- **Length outlier rule has only 6 mock examples.** Don't override knowledge with it.
- **L15 / L20 topics have no mock precedent.** ResNet, cross-entropy, VAE, backprop questions — apply rule 1 (absolutism) and rule 3 (adjacency) confidently; treat the rest with caution.

## Final word

If you're applying the playbook to more than 5 questions, you are under-prepared on the topic, not running out of luck. The rules are calibrated to the mock; the real exam will diverge. Knowledge first, patterns last.

---

Full audit: [[mcq-pattern-evidence]]. Full playbook: [[mcq-playbook]].

content.text:
All three artifacts written and cross-checked. Summary:

**Files created** in `40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/`:
- `mcq-pattern-evidence.md` — per-question 3-layer tagging table, frequency counts, class-stratified pattern strength
- `mcq-playbook.md` — main artifact: 30-second sequence, per-pattern rule cards (A1/A2/A3 HIGH; B1/B2/B3 MEDIUM-HIGH; C1 brittle), class overrides, honest "where this fails" section
- `mcq-playbook-cheatsheet.md` — one-page distillation, 5 ranked moves + class quick-reference

**Key findings beyond what's in the existing style-guide:**

1. **Absolutism elimination** is the single most reliable rule: 10 distractor instances of "perfectly/completely/all/no" across 8 questions, **zero** in correct answers. Classical Millman; the prof has not defended against it.
2. **Symmetric-swap structure extends to 14/25 questions** (style-guide noted 8). Correct answer is in the swap pair 14/14 times. Operationalised as "drop non-swap options first".
3. **Length-outlier rule**: 6/6 outliers wrong on the mock — n is small but hit-rate clean. MEDIUM-HIGH.
4. **Position-C bias is real but brittle**: C=44%, B=8% (z≈+2.2 / −2.0). Flagged this contradicts the style-guide's "no obvious positional bias" claim — kept it as last-resort tiebreaker only because n=25 is too small to trust.
5. **Class divergence matters**: discrimination MCQs (40% of the exam) are length-balanced; surface tells fail. Application MCQs are 100% swap-grids; you must know the rule. Computation MCQs have no surface tells. Recall MCQs are where the classical "longest = right" partially holds.
6. **Demoted rules** (transparently flagged in §4): vanilla "longest = right" is near-null (52% top-half rate vs 50% baseline), odd-one-out is bidirectional, vocabulary echo too noisy.

The playbook explicitly states it buys ~1–2 marks of EV at most and degrades on L15/L20 topics that have no mock precedent.
